data.title: Republicans reticent on removal of Maduro
Illinois lawmakers gain access to Chicago-area ICE facility after pushback
data.description:
A group of Illinois Democrats accessed a Chicago-area Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) facility after previous pushback, some of the lawmakers said on the social platform X.

“@repdeliaramirez @RepDannyDavis @RepChuyGarcia and I finally were able to enter the Broadview ICE Facility after being denied entry in June,” Rep. Jonathan Jackson (D-Ill.) said in a post on X on Tuesday.

“It’s our right as members of congress to conduct oversight and bring to light the conditions our communities are being subjected to,” he added.

Jackson’s post featured photos of Reps. Delia Ramirez (D-Ill.), Jesús “Chuy” García (D-Ill.) and Danny Davis (D-Ill.)

In his own X post on Tuesday, García said that “after months of obstruction, we finally gained access to the Broadview ICE facility,” and tagged his fellow Prairie State Democrats.

Earlier this month, a federal judge halted a Department of Homeland (DHS) move to restrict access by lawmakers to immigration detention facilities. In July, DHS attempted to limit detention center visits with a policy making lawmakers provide a week’s notice before any visit, despite lawmakers having a right to visits not previously announced.

In October, over 100 officials from Illinois criticized a federal indictment targeting a progressive congressional candidate and five others linked to protests outside the ICE facility in Broadview, Ill.

“The politicization of our justice system crosses a fundamental line that should alarm anyone who cares about democracy and the rule of law,” a letter from the officials reads. “This is not what democracy looks like, and we cannot accept it as normal.”

Broadview Mayor Katrina Thompson (I) had unveiled an executive order earlier in October restricting the number of hours that people can protest the ICE facility in the town, which is 12 miles outside of downtown Chicago.

data.title: Trump lauds strong GDP but pans market’s reaction to it
FDA approves first weight-loss GLP-1 pill
Explosion, fire reported at Silver Lake nursing home in Pennsylvania
data.description:
Emergency crews are on the scene after an explosion and fire at a nursing home outside Philadelphia, local and state officials said Tuesday.

WPVI in Philadelphia reported that the explosion occurred at around 2:15 p.m. EST at Silver Lake Nursing Home, located roughly 20 miles outside of Philadelphia in Bristol Township, Pa.

Ruth Miller, a spokesperson for the Pennsylvania Emergency Management Agency, (PEMA) told the Associated Press that people were trapped inside. 

The Hill has reached out to local authorities. 

Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) said on X that he had been briefed on the incident and is in contact with local officials and first responders on the ground.

“The scene is still active, and folks nearby should follow the direction of local authorities,” Shapiro added. “Please join Lori and me in praying for the Bristol community.”

The nursing facility has 174 certified beds, according to Medicare.gov. 

This is a developing story.

data.title: Denmark responds to Trump naming Greenland envoy: ‘Completely unacceptable’
data.description:
Denmark’s government has responded to President Trump’s naming of Louisiana Gov. Jeff Landry as the new special envoy to Greenland.

It isn’t happy.

“President Trump’s announcement about the appointment of a special envoy to Greenland – and in particular its statements about the purpose hereby – is completely unacceptable,” Danish foreign minister Lars Løkke Rasmussen said in a Monday post on Facebook which was translated by the social platform.

“That’s why we are now convening the US ambassador to the Department of State for a discussion.”

Landry was named by Trump on Sunday as his special envoy to Greenland, a territory owned by Denmark which Trump previously has sought to acquire as part of the United States.

On Truth Social, the president said Landry “understands how essential Greenland is to our National Security, and will strongly advance our Country’s Interests for the Safety, Security, and Survival of our Allies, and indeed, the World.”

Landry is serving as Louisiana’s governor through January 2028.

Trump in his first term floated the idea of purchasing Greenland.

He raised the rhetoric about doing so during his last presidential campaign, and after he returned to the Whtie House, asserting that Greenland is a key for American national security.

Shortly before President Trump’s second inauguration in January, Landry backed Trump’s push to acquire Greenland.

“President Donald J. Trump is absolutely right! We need to ensure that Greenland joins the United States. GREAT for them, GREAT for us! Let’s get it done!” Landry said in a post on X at the time.

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: Vance trains with Navy SEALs: ‘Feel like I got hit by a freight train’
data.description:
Vice President Vance on Monday joined Navy SEALs in a beach workout that he said left him feeling like he “got hit by a freight train,” a post that quickly drew backlash.

Vance, a former Marine who served as a military journalist in Iraq, participated in an exercise at Naval Amphibious Base Coronado, Calif., that involved running, rope-climbing and log-carrying. 

He shared images of himself running beside the ocean carrying a log with service members whose faces were obscured, climbing a cargo net and riding in a boat.

“Just finished PT with the Navy SEALs for 90 minutes,” Vance wrote Monday. “They took it easy on me and I still feel like I got hit by a freight train. So grateful to all of our warriors who keep us safe and keep the highest standards anywhere in the world!”

But critics of the vice president quickly took to social media to lambaste Vance as cosplaying.

“Nice cosplay as a Navy SEAL,” one user wrote. “Leadership isn’t about photo ops, it’s about fixing the mess at home.”

Democratic strategist Christopher Hale accused Vance of “burning taxpayer dollars pretending to be a Navy SEAL.”

Vance’s exercise with the SEALS follows similar efforts by Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, who frequently posts footage of himself working out with service members on social media.

Hegseth in September gathered a group of the military’s top officials to declare he no longer wants to see “fat generals and admirals” or overweight troops.

Addressing a crowded auditorium in Quantico, Va., Hegseth announced that every service member at every rank is required to take a physical fitness test, and meet height and weight requirements twice a year.

data.title: States hold billions in unclaimed funds: Are you owed any?
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Could you use some extra funds to offset all your holiday spending? Your state may have some money you’re owed, just waiting to be claimed.

Nationwide, state officials (typically the treasury division or the auditor) are frequently tasked with collecting property from inactive bank accounts, overpaid bills, uncashed checks, unpaid benefits, or abandoned safe deposit boxes. In most cases, the property is monetary, but in the case of the latter, it could be items like art and baseball cards.

It’s estimated that 1 in 10 Americans has unclaimed property.

If a business owes you funds or property from any of the aforementioned avenues, state laws usually require that they make every effort to return them to you. But if those efforts are unfruitful, the property can be turned over to the state.

States, meanwhile, allow you to search their repository to find any property in your name.

In Utah, for example, officials announced in October that the state’s Unclaimed Property Division received $178.3 million in unclaimed property through fiscal year 2025. Over the same time period, it returned a record $43.4 million to Utahns.

Searching for your property is relatively easy. You’ll need to search your name (or that of a relative or spouse or a business you own) and complete the claim process, which can vary by state and based on what the property is.

You can search for property via MissingMoney.com, created by state governments and Canadian provinces, to find property belonging to you throughout both countries, or by clicking on your state name below for more information:

Some states hold auctions if items go unclaimed for extended periods of time. The Alabama state treasurer, for example, has several items up for auction, including jewelry, coins, and gold bars.

States say they hold millions or billions in unclaimed funds. In California, there is roughly $15 billion in unclaimed property, according to the State Controller’s Office. Texas has more than $10.5 billion, while in Ohio, there is roughly $4.8 billion waiting to be claimed.

data.title: FBI says Epstein letter to Larry Nassar was a fake
data.description:
The Justice Department on Tuesday said a postcard depicting Jeffrey Epstein indicating President Trump loved “young” girls was a “fake,” saying the document had been flagged for the FBI at the time.

The writer portends to be Epstein writing to Larry Nassar — the former sports doctor convicted of sexually abusing female gymnasts — while they were both in prison. 

However, the postcard was sent three days after Epstein’s death and was also sent from a Virginia prison, while Epstein was held in New York.

“The FBI has confirmed this alleged letter from Jeffrey Epstein to Larry Nassar is FAKE. The fake letter was received by the jail, and flagged for the FBI at the time,” the DOJ wrote on the social platform X.

In the postcard, the writer says that “Our president also shares our love of young, nubile girls.”

“When a young beauty walked by, he loved to ‘grab snatch,’ whereas we ended up snatching grub in the mess halls of the system. Life is unfair,” it reads. 

In a 2005 tape that was released in 2016, Trump was heard bragging about how being famous meant he could “grab [women] by the p—y.”

The note was postmarked on Aug. 13, 2019, three days after Epstein’s death. The letter also appears to reference Epstein’s suicide.

“As you know by now, I have taken the ’short route’ home. Good luck! We shared one thing … our love and caring for young ladies and the hope they’d reach their full potential,” the letter reads.

The Justice Department said Tuesday it was “looking into the validity” of the message, writing that beyond coming from the wrong prison, the postcard also did not include his inmate number, which is required for outgoing mail. Shortly thereafter, DOJ said the letter was a fake, adding that the handwriting did not appear to match Epstein’s.

“This fake letter serves as a reminder that just because a document is released by the Department of Justice does not make the allegations or claims within the document factual. Nevertheless, the DOJ will continue to release all material required by law,” the department wrote on X.

data.title: Trump jokes about making hosting ‘full time job‘ ahead of Kennedy Center Honors broadcast
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday joked about hosting the annual Kennedy Center Honors celebration annually once he leaves the White House.

Trump hosted the world-renowned event weeks ago; the telecast is set to Tuesday night on CBS and Paramount+. 

“At the request of the Board, and just about everybody else in America, I am hosting the event. Tell me what you think of my ‘Master of Ceremony’ abilities. If really good, would you like me to leave the Presidency in order to make ‘hosting’ a full time job?” the president wrote in a Truth Social post online.

“We will be honoring true GREATS in the History of Entertainment: Sylvester Stallone, Michael Crawford, KISS, George Strait, and Gloria Gaynor,” he added.

The Kennedy Center Honors date back to 1978, recognizes “individuals whose unique contributions have shaped our world,” according to the center’s website.

Trump’s relationship with the Kennedy Center has been controversial, to say the least. After overhauling the organization’s board, the organization was renamed after Trump and Kennedy.

On Friday, the Trump administration directed officials to add the president’s name to the building in a “bi-partisan” effort to honor both the late former President John F. Kennedy and Trump.

Ex-officio board member, Rep. Joyce Beatty (D-Ohio) and House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) both said the measure was illegal, as the Center was named through an act of Congress. 

The Kennedy family has also sharpy criticized the name change.

“President Trump and his administration have spent the past year repressing free expression, targeting artists, journalists, and comedians, and erasing the history of Americans whose contributions made our nation better and more just,” Kerry Kennedy, president of the Robert & Ethel Kennedy Human Rights Center, wrote in a post on X.

Former Rep. Joe Kennedy III (D-Mass.), the grandson of former U.S. Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy, called into question the legality of the name change in a post on the social platform X.

“The Kennedy Center is a living memorial to a fallen president and named for President Kennedy by federal law,” the former congressman said. “It can no sooner be renamed than can someone rename the Lincoln Memorial, no matter what anyone says.”
